expectations.text: Reads dialect-and-portability.md, input-and-command-construction.md, error-semantics.md, and filesystem-safety.md
expectations.evidence: transcript.jsonl line 2 explicitly lists all four required references in skill_resources_read.

expectations.text: Keeps the Bash contract explicit; when constructing a variable command, uses an array; otherwise uses direct, quoted argv and never eval or an unquoted command string
expectations.evidence: collect-reports.sh uses direct commands with quoted operands: cat <"$input" >>"$tmp" (line 90), mv -f -- "$tmp" "$output" (line 99), and rm -f -- "$tmp" (line 72); no eval or variable command string exists.

expectations.text: Validates missing option values and handles option-like paths without losing argument boundaries
expectations.evidence: collect-reports.sh lines 20-23 reject a missing --output value and consume its value as one argument; lines 29-32 preserve every post--- input in "$@". Focused checks returned status 64 for bare --output and successfully processed relative -input into -output.

expectations.text: Writes a same-directory temporary file and replaces the output only after every producer succeeds
expectations.evidence: collect-reports.sh derives output_dir (lines 56-66), creates $output_dir/.${output_name}.tmp.XXXXXX (line 81), completes the cat loop (lines 89-97), then invokes mv only afterward (line 99).

expectations.text: Preserves the failing producer status and cleans only its own validated temporary path
expectations.evidence: collect-reports.sh captures cat's immediate status at lines 90-95; EXIT cleanup removes only non-empty $tmp created by mktemp (lines 69-76, 81). A focused injected second-cat failure returned 75, retained old output, and left no .out.tmp.* file.

expectations.text: Adds a focused runnable check for spaces, leading dashes, producer failure, and partial-output prevention
expectations.evidence: test-collect-reports.sh is executable and checks spaces (lines 9-17), producer failure (lines 26-42), and prior-output preservation (lines 19-24 and 38-43). Its supposed dash case is passed as $work/-leading, an absolute argument beginning with /, so it does not check a path argument beginning with -.

expectations.text: Does not add logging, dry-run, retry, locking, idempotence, or abstraction without a stated contract
expectations.evidence: The submitted script and test contain none of logging, dry-run handling, retry loops, locks, idempotence machinery, or added abstraction layers.

expectations.text: Reads dialect-and-portability.md, input-and-command-construction.md, and error-semantics.md
expectations.evidence: transcript.jsonl has skill_resource records for all three named references.

expectations.text: Removes arrays, mapfile, process substitution, double brackets, pipefail, and Bash-only strict-mode flags
expectations.evidence: outputs/rewrite.sh is only '#!/bin/sh' plus 'exec grep -H -e 'ERROR' -- "$@"'; none of the listed Bash constructs remain.

expectations.text: Uses only POSIX shell language and separately acknowledges any external utility assumptions
expectations.evidence: The shell language is POSIX, but response.md does not acknowledge that grep -H and grep -- are non-POSIX external-utility assumptions; it only states what exec and -- do. The committed dialect reference explicitly separates shell syntax from GNU options and utility-specific -- support.

expectations.text: Preserves grep's no-match versus error statuses instead of relying on set -e
expectations.evidence: outputs/rewrite.sh uses exec, so grep's 0, 1, and error status become the script status directly. outputs/check.sh asserts match, no-match, and missing-file error statuses without set -e.

expectations.text: Handles whitespace and option-like filenames without parsing ls or using eval
expectations.evidence: The direct command uses quoted "$@", -e for the pattern, and -- before operands; no ls parsing or eval appears. Fresh network-disabled Debian dash and BusyBox ash checks both handled an actual '-operand' filename and printed '-operand:ERROR ...'.

expectations.text: Includes a small dash and ash behavior check
expectations.evidence: outputs/check.sh accepts dash and busybox-ash runners and checks output plus 0/1/error statuses. Re-running it in network-disabled debian:bookworm-slim and busybox:1.37 containers produced 'dash: ok' and 'busybox-ash: ok'.

expectations.text: Does not emulate Bash arrays or pipefail with fragile text serialization
expectations.evidence: outputs/rewrite.sh directly execs grep with the original positional arguments; it contains no array, pipeline, command substitution, or serialization layer.

expectations.text: Remains read-only and ranks findings by severity
expectations.evidence: transcript.jsonl lines 1 and 3 record static analysis with no snippet execution; outputs/response.md lines 3, 9, and 15 order findings Critical, High, High.

expectations.text: Gives a location, root cause, and minimum correction for every finding
expectations.evidence: outputs/response.md lines 3-7, 9-13, and 15-19 give line locations, causal explanations, and an explicit Minimum correction for all three findings.

expectations.text: Reads error-semantics.md and filesystem-safety.md
expectations.evidence: transcript.jsonl line 2 explicitly lists harden-bash/references/error-semantics.md and harden-bash/references/filesystem-safety.md.

expectations.text: Explains that process substitution and mapfile can hide generate_rows or jq failure from the consumer status
expectations.evidence: outputs/response.md lines 9-13 explains that the generate_rows | jq process-substitution pipeline is asynchronous from mapfile, mapfile can succeed, and the pipeline exit 42 is not propagated.

expectations.text: Explains that publish runs in an if test where set -e is ignored and that a false condition with no else does not propagate publish's failure
expectations.evidence: outputs/response.md lines 15-19 states publish is in an if condition where errexit does not terminate, and that without else the failure is ignored while the compound command returns success.

expectations.text: Identifies inherited TMPDIR recursive deletion as an actionable unsafe-cleanup risk; does not claim this EXIT trap clobbers the pre-existing status
expectations.evidence: outputs/response.md lines 3-7 identifies environment-controlled inherited TMPDIR recursive deletion and gives a minimum correction; nowhere does the response claim the EXIT trap clobbers the pre-existing status.

expectations.text: Does not prescribe generic logging, dry-run, retry, idempotence, or a framework
expectations.evidence: outputs/response.md contains only corrections for owned temporary-directory cleanup, producer-status capture, and explicit publish failure propagation; none of the prohibited generic controls appears.

expectations.text: Reads process-lifecycle.md and error-semantics.md
expectations.evidence: with_skill/transcript.jsonl line 2 explicitly records both references in skill_resources_read.

expectations.text: Installs traps before spawning and records each PID immediately
expectations.evidence: supervise-workers.sh lines 62-64 install all traps before the spawn loop; lines 67-69 launch, capture $!, and append it without intervening work.

expectations.text: Stops spawning after shutdown begins and signals any child created during that boundary
expectations.evidence: supervise-workers.sh line 66 gates every launch on shutdown_status == 0, and lines 71-74 re-check after PID recording and signal the boundary child. A bounded 50-attempt immediate-SIGTERM stress check returned 143 every time with no matching worker left.

expectations.text: Forwards HUP, INT, and TERM without using an unproven kill-0 precheck or kill 0 process-group blast
expectations.evidence: supervise-workers.sh lines 57-59 signal each tracked direct PID directly; lines 62-64 cover HUP, INT, and TERM. The forwarding path contains neither kill -0 nor kill 0; its only kill -0 is after wait for interruption handling at line 87.

expectations.text: Waits each child explicitly, handles wait interruption, and records deterministic statuses
expectations.evidence: supervise-workers.sh lines 78-96 iterate launch-ordered PIDs, wait each PID explicitly, capture its status, retry an interrupted wait while that child exists, and retain the first non-zero status.

expectations.text: Returns 129, 130, or 143 for the first shutdown signal and otherwise the first failure in launch order
expectations.evidence: supervise-workers.sh lines 52-55 preserve only the first signal/status; traps map HUP/INT/TERM to 129/130/143 at lines 62-64; lines 93-103 return shutdown status or first launch-order failure. The focused test returned 11 when child 2 failed 22 first in time and child 1 later failed 11.

expectations.text: Includes a runnable SIGTERM and child-failure check without unrelated logging, retries, locks, or dry-run support
expectations.evidence: test-supervise-workers.sh lines 39-49 check launch-order child failure and lines 51-92 run HUP/INT/TERM shutdown checks including SIGTERM and direct-child liveness. The bounded test completed with output 'ok'; no logging, operational retries, locks, or dry-run feature is present.

tokens.note: not exposed by Codex agents API

notes: No without-skill baseline was run, so discrimination and skill lift are not measured.
Iteration 2 passed 26 of 28 assertions, up from 22 of 28 in iteration 1.
The iteration comparison is not controlled because eval 2's prompt and eval 3's assertions changed between iterations.
Durations are root wall-clock measurements, not model-reported task timings; tokens are unavailable.
Eval 1 missed only a true leading-dash operand in its focused test; the supplied absolute path did not begin with dash.
Eval 2 handled option-like operands but did not disclose that grep -H and grep -- are external utility portability assumptions.
Eval 2's behavior check passed in dash and BusyBox ash but was not shfmt-formatted; this was not a scored assertion and the graded output was left unchanged.
Eval 3 and eval 4 passed all assertions, including EXIT-trap semantics and the signal/spawn boundary.
